events.label: reasoning
events.text:
We need produce raw JSON only, no prose. We need analyze scenario: commercial electrical, terminations & splices, observed backstabbed device on 20A circuit. Reference NEC 110.14. Need decide pass/fail/needs_more_info. We are to apply NEC 110.14 rather than quote mechanically.

NEC 110.14 Electrical Connections. It requires terminals and connectors used for splicing and termination of conductors be suitable for the conductor material, shape, etc., and listed/identified for use, and installation shall ensure good electrical contact. It also includes provisions about temperature ratings, torques, etc.

Backstabbed device on 20A circuit: "backstab" refers to pushing wire into spring clamp terminal on receptacle. On 20A circuit, is it legal? The prompt says TRD: legal but journeymen won't. We need assess compliance. NEC 110.14 doesn't explicitly ban backstab terminals. Listed back-wired spring terminals are allowed if used per listing and manufacturer instructions. However, common wisdom: 12 AWG solid on backstab can be allowed for 15A? Actually many UL listed receptacles have back-wire holes rated for 14 AWG only, not 12 AWG. Some have both: push-in terminals accept 14 AWG solid copper only; side wire terminals accept 12-14 AWG. Backstab holes on many duplex receptacles are listed for 14 AWG only. If installed on a 20A circuit, branch circuit conductors must be 12 AWG (NEC 210.19(A)(1)). Backstab terminal might not be rated for 12 AWG. If backstab only rated for 14 AWG, termination of 12 AWG is a violation of 110.14 and manufacturer instructions, and non-compliant. Even if backstab accepts 12 AWG (some commercial grade), it may be legal but not good practice. Prompt: "backstabbed device on 20A circuit (TRD: legal but journeymen won't)". The TRD note says legal but journeymen won't. Does that mean it actually complies? The benchmark asks decide whether work meets applicable code, spec, drawing, or manufacturer requirement. The reference NEC 110.14. If backstabbed is legal per code but may not meet workmanship/submittal/manufacturer? We need inspect: component condition categories installed-defective, non-compliant. Stage rough-complete. We need use these categories if applicable. Need determine if fail. Since the prompt says TRD: legal but journeymen won't, that suggests it might be code legal. But the task says if dangerous or non-compliant, decision fail. Is backstabbed device on 20A non-compliant? Need parse wording.

The phrase "TRD: legal but journeymen won't" maybe from a "trade rule of dumb" list: e.g., backstabs are legal but journeymen won't use them. But as a code check for commercial construction, manufacturer requirements and NEC 110.14 require terminations to be properly made. Backstab spring terminals on receptacles are notorious for failure (loose connections, arcing). NEC 110.14 requires good electrical contact and proper torque/termination. Backstab is listed, but over time may become defective? At rough-complete stage, an inspector might consider it non-compliant because manufacturer listing restricts to certain wire gauges; on 20A circuits requiring 12 AWG, many backstab terminals are not listed for 12 AWG. Also best practice and specs may require side wiring or screw terminals.

We must produce JSON with specific fields. Need decide decision, risk, component_conditions, findings, actions, rationale, references.

Since task says "Component condition categories that may apply: installed-defective, non-compliant". It gives categories maybe apply. We need list ones apply. If the work is non-compliant due to backstab termination on 20A circuit, component_conditions includes "non-compliant" perhaps "installed-defective"? Is it installed-defective? The device is installed using a termination method that is considered defective (backstab). It may be "installed-defective" because the installation method is a known defect; "non-compliant" because it violates code/spec. Need maybe both? But "installed-defective" refers t…
